target_tools.description:
Execute composable JavaScript to search Gmail, conversation memory, and Twitter in one call. Use return for output.
Available helpers: - search(query, opts?) → SearchResult[] — semantic/keyword/hybrid search over memory. Returns { sourceId, snippet, score, userName?, createdAt?, thread? }. opts: { mode?: "vector"|"keyword"|"hybrid", maxResults?, minScore? } - getMessages(opts?) → MessageResult[] — fetch messages by filter. Returns { id, userName, role, text, createdAt }. opts: { user?, after?, before?, role?, limit?, order? } - semanticFilter(msgs, concept, threshold?) → ScoredMessageResult[] — filter chat messages by meaning (pre-computed embeddings) - filterBySimilarity(items, concept, opts?) → {item, similarity}[] — generic semantic filter for any array - gmail.search(query, {max?}) → [{id, date, from, subject, labels, messageCount}] - gmail.messagesSearch(query, {max?, includeBody?}) → GmailMessage[] - gmail.get(messageId) → GmailMessage — { id, threadId, from, to, subject, date, body, labels, attachments } - gmail.thread(threadId, {full?}) → {id, messages} - twitter.feed(username?, page?) → XML timeline - twitter.search(query, page?, sort?) → XML results
Use Promise.all() for parallel searches. Keyword query syntax: "word word" (AND), "word OR word", "exact phrase", -word (exclude).

I'm sorry about your dad. And I want you to know — the fact that you're looking at this stuff and asking questions means you're not failing at this. Executor work is genuinely hard, especially when you're grieving at the same time.

Let me give you the single most important thing first, because it's actually *good* news:

**Stepped-up basis.** When you inherit investments, the IRS resets their cost to whatever they were worth on the day your dad died. So if he bought GE at $10/share thirty years ago, that doesn't matter anymore — your cost basis is whatever GE was worth in October. This means **you can sell some or all of that GE position right now with very little capital gains tax.** That window is a gift. The longer GE moves away from that October price (up *or* down), the more it changes.

So to answer your question directly: yes, 39% in one stock is genuinely risky, and you actually have a rare, clean opportunity to fix it without a big tax hit.

**Now here's the triage — what matters when:**

🔴 **Now-ish (next few weeks):**
- Talk to the Edward Jones advisor, but go in knowing: EJ advisors typically work on commission. He might know useful things about your dad's accounts and intentions, so *listen* — but don't sign anything or move money based on that call alone.
- Get a CPA if you don't have one. Your dad's final tax return is due April 2026. The estate may also need its own return.

🟡 **Soon (next couple months):**
- Look at the Fidelity account. We can go through it together like we did this one.
- Think about diversifying that GE position while the stepped-up basis is fresh.

🟢 **Can wait:**
- Optimizing the portfolio structure (the duplicate index funds, bond allocation, etc.). None of that is urgent.

One question that changes a lot: **do you have a CPA or financial advisor of your own?** Not your dad's EJ guy — someone working for *you*, ideally a fee-only fiduciary (meaning they're legally required to act in your interest, not earning commissions). If you don't have one, that's the single most important hire right now. With three accounts and estate taxes to navigate, you want a professional in your corner.

What's the Fidelity account situation — do you have login access, or is that part of the executor mess too?
